This is why I'm here. Not running meetings about assessment metrics. Not reviewing other teachers' lesson plans. This—watching a kid find his voice, being there for that moment. Lucia thinks I'm settling. Maybe I am. But there are worse things to settle for than this.

Mama made me empanadas when I got home because she remembered it was the first day. She can barely stand at the stove that long anymore but she did it anyway. I tried to tell her she didn't have to and she just waved me off and said something about how her daughter deserves a celebration.

Sofia texted to say she's doing better. Didn't ask for anything, just wanted to share. That's new. Maybe things are shifting. Maybe I helped get her there, or maybe she got herself there. Either way, I smiled at my phone like an idiot for a good minute.

Nudge: "What do you think made the difference?"

Spent the morning glazing the new bowl series — that deep blue with copper undertones I've been chasing for months. Watched the light shift across the wet surface and forgot about everything else for a while. This is why I do this. The moment when the color catches and something ordinary becomes something worth looking at.

Then Mama called to ask if I could take her to the pharmacy. Of course I said yes. Packed up the brushes, washed my hands, drove her across town. We stopped for knafeh after and she held my hand in the car on the way back, said she's grateful I came home.

I am too, most days. But I also think about how I used to have whole weeks where nobody needed me for anything, and I could just make things until my hands hurt. I don't know if I miss that or if I just think I should miss it.

My cousin Nadia asked me to paint something for her new apartment. Not commissioned — just asked, the way family asks. Like my time and materials are a gift I owe her because we share blood. I said yes because what else do you say when your aunt is standing right there and Nadia's going through a divorce and everyone's been worried about her.

I don't even know what she wants. Something cheerful, she said. Blue maybe. Something to look at in the morning.

So now I'm supposed to make something cheerful and blue on top of the three workshop sessions I'm prepping, the gallery order that's due next week, and driving Mama to her appointments. And the worst part is I can't even be properly upset because Nadia's having a hard year and I should want to help. I do want to help. I just also want someone to ask me what I need instead of what I can give.

Had a glass of wine on the balcony after everyone left tonight. The city noise, the warm air, the taste of it — that small hour was the best part of my whole week. And I feel stupid admitting that because it's nothing, it's just sitting there, but everything else is for other people and that was just for me.

Nudge: "What would you need right now if someone asked?"

Response: I think I'd want someone to take over the pharmacy runs for a week. Just one week where I don't have to be anywhere at a specific time. I'd finish the gallery order early and maybe drive to the Dead Sea with Reem and Dana, just for a day. Float in the water, eat too much, laugh at nothing. Come back tired in a good way instead of the way I usually come back tired.

It sounds selfish when I write it out. But I used to do things like that more often, and I was better company. I was a better artist too, I think. Less resentful. I don't want to become someone who counts favors.

Workshop went well today. Eight kids, ages seven to twelve. We made pinch pots and one girl named Sana made hers into a turtle. She was so proud she carried it to her mother with both hands like it was made of glass.

My brother Tariq said he might take Mama to the pharmacy on Thursday. We'll see if he actually follows through.

Nudge: "What happens if he doesn't follow through?"

Can't sleep. Tariq texted to say he has a work thing Thursday and can I cover after all. Of course I can. Of course I will.

I keep thinking about what Reem said last month, that I should just say no sometimes and see what happens. But she doesn't have a mother who needs her, and she doesn't have a brother who conveniently forgets his commitments, and she doesn't understand that if I don't do it then it just doesn't get done or Mama has to take a taxi alone and feel like a burden.

The thing is I don't even want to stop helping. I want to stop being the only one who helps. There's a difference but it gets lost in translation when I try to explain it to anyone in my family.

Started a new sketch tonight though. Something abstract, just shapes and warmth. No one asked for it. It's ugly so far but it's mine.

Finished Nadia's painting yesterday. Took it to her apartment and she cried when she saw it — not because it was good, I think, but because she needed someone to do something soft for her and I was the one who did it. We sat on her empty floor surrounded by moving boxes and she told me things about her marriage I didn't know. How small she'd felt. How invisible.

I stayed longer than I planned. Made her tea with the one pot she'd unpacked. Told her about the residency I turned down two years ago and how I sometimes wonder if I made the right call coming back. She said at least you had a call to make. At least you have something that's yours.

That stayed with me on the drive home. I do have something that's mine. The studio, the clay, the color mixing — all of it. Even when I'm tired and resentful and covered in glaze at midnight, I'm still making things that didn't exist before I made them.

Treated myself to good coffee this morning. The expensive kind, with cardamom. Sat in the garden and drank it slow while the light came up. Small luxuries. I'm learning they're not small at all — they're how I keep going. Without the good coffee and the Dead Sea trips and the wine on the balcony, I'd hollow out. I need those things as much as my family needs the pharmacy runs. Maybe that's okay.

End of the year crunch is starting to show. Had three classes back to back this morning and by fourth period I was basically on autopilot. The juniors are doing their neighborhood oral history projects, which is the one part of the semester I still genuinely look forward to, but even that felt like pushing a boulder today.

Stayed late grading because I refuse to bring that stack home again. The custodian, Marco, stopped by and we talked for twenty minutes about his kid's Little League team. Probably the best conversation I had all day. Sometimes the building feels more like mine after everyone leaves. Quieter. No one asking me to fill out forms or attend alignment meetings.

Came home, heated up leftover chili, and sat in the garage for a while not really doing anything with the lathe. Just sitting. The dog parked herself at my feet. That was enough for tonight.

Could not sleep. Kept replaying the staff meeting from this afternoon. Reynolds stood up there with her slides about "instructional coherence" and basically told us the district is adopting a packaged curriculum for all social studies classes starting next fall. Everyone just nodded. I raised my hand and asked what happens to the electives I built. She said we would "discuss individual cases" later. That is code for no.

Twelve years of that program. I designed every unit, every field trip route, every community partner relationship. And some vendor's binder is supposed to replace it because test scores dipped two points in a grade level I do not even teach. I should have pushed harder in the meeting. Or maybe I should not have said anything at all. I do not know which one sits worse.

Nudge: "What stopped you from pushing harder?"

Response: Honestly, the room. Everyone had already accepted it. I looked around and realized I was the only one who cared enough to object, and that made me feel old and stubborn rather than right.

Had the juniors present their oral history drafts today and it reminded me exactly why I do not want to hand this over to a shrink-wrapped curriculum. Daniela interviewed her grandmother about the Vanport flood. She brought in a photograph she had never seen before, one her grandmother kept in a shoebox for sixty years. The whole class went quiet.

You cannot buy that in a textbook package. You cannot script it. It comes from giving kids room to chase something that matters to them and getting out of the way. I told Daniela after class that her project was one of the best I have seen in nearly three decades, and she looked genuinely shocked, like no one had told her she was good at anything academic before.

Drove home with the windows down even though it was drizzling. Put on the Coltrane record Linda used to play on Friday evenings. Poured a glass of wine. Felt like myself for the first time all week. Whatever happens with the curriculum decision, today was proof that my way of teaching works. That has to count for something.

Reynolds pulled me aside after school and offered me a role as "curriculum liaison" for the new program. Basically wants me to help implement the thing I spent the last week arguing against. I said I would think about it but the whole walk to the parking lot felt like swallowing something sharp.

Nudge: "What part of saying yes scares you most?"

Been thinking about the liaison offer all weekend. My daughter called Sunday and I mentioned it. She said take it, that it is better to be at the table than shouting from outside. She is probably right from a strategic perspective. But something about it feels like agreeing that the packaged curriculum is acceptable as long as I get a title and a small say in how it rolls out.

I keep thinking about the first year I built the civic history elective. Nobody approved it. I just wrote the proposal, walked it into the old principal's office, and made my case. He let me try it for one semester. That was 2013. It has run every year since. The idea that I would now become the person helping dismantle what I created on my own terms feels backwards.

But I am also fifty-seven. I have maybe eight years left before retirement. Part of me wonders if fighting this is just stubbornness dressed up as principle. Linda would have told me to pick my battles. She was better at knowing which hills were worth it. I keep reaching for that kind of clarity and coming up empty.

Nudge: "If Linda said take it, would you listen?"

Told Reynolds no on Friday. She took it well enough, said the door stays open. I do not think it does, but that is fine. Spent the weekend not thinking about school for the first time in weeks. Built a cutting board out of walnut and maple for my neighbor's birthday. Spent four hours on the glue-up alone because I kept adjusting the pattern. Nobody was timing me.

Saturday morning I went to the farmers market and ran into two former students, both from the civic history elective, one from 2016 and one from 2019. The 2016 kid, well she is twenty-five now, told me she works for the city planning office and still thinks about the neighborhood mapping project. I did not bring up the curriculum stuff. But it sat with me the whole drive home.

Graded the last round of oral history presentations tonight. Gave Daniela's project full marks and wrote her a recommendation letter she did not ask for. I figure if the program disappears next year, at least this cohort got the real version. The garage smelled like fresh-cut walnut when I came inside. The dog was asleep on the sawdust pile again. Quiet house, but not an empty one.

Spent the morning reviewing the pitch deck for the Malmstrom account. Anna prepared it, and it's solid work—maybe better than what I would have done. That should feel good. Mostly it does. But I caught myself wanting to change things anyway, just to put my fingerprints on it.

Held back. Let her present it. We got the work.

There's this line I'm trying to walk. I built this company because I wanted to do things my way, make choices that made sense to me. But somewhere along the way "my way" has to include other people's ways too, or I'm just running a one-man show with extra chairs.

Drove home through the first real snow of the season. The city looked good.

Nudge: "What made it hard to hold back? Was there something specific about her version that felt different from what you would have done?"

Response: It wasn't that her version was wrong. It was actually the right tone for this client—softer, more collaborative than my usual approach. I think what made it hard was realizing I might have been doing it wrong all these years. Or not wrong, but... narrower than I needed to be.

I keep thinking about what it means to build something. I started this so I could make decisions. But decisions aren't just about me anymore. And that's uncomfortable in a way I didn't expect.

Board meeting for the industry association today. Three hours of people who've never started anything telling founders how to run our businesses. Compliance updates. Risk mitigation frameworks. Standard recommendations for standard companies.

I know they mean well. I know some of this matters.

But I sat there thinking about all the things I could be doing instead. Actual work. Actual decisions that affect actual outcomes.

Left early. Probably shouldn't have.

Marcus called today. My old business partner, from the agency days. He's still there—made partner eventually, corner office, the whole thing. Wanted to know if I'd consider coming back. Not as an employee, as a "strategic partner." They'd acquire the consultancy, I'd run a division, get their resources, their clients, their benefits package.

I said I'd think about it, which is what you say when you don't want to say no immediately.

But I already know.

The thing is, Marcus sounds tired. He sounds like someone who traded the thing that matters for the thing that lasts. Stability. Predictability. A clear path to retirement with a good pension. And I don't judge him for it—we're different people, wanted different things.

What I can't stop thinking about is whether I'm fooling myself. The consultancy had a rough year. We lost the Bergqvist account in March, spent too much on that software project that went nowhere. I'm fine, we're fine, but it's not the growth trajectory I imagined when I started.

Maybe Marcus is right and I'm just stubborn. Maybe there's a version of partnership that would give me what I need without taking what I can't give up.

Or maybe I just can't stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again.

Went for a run after the call. Didn't help as much as I wanted it to.

Nudge: "You mentioned the rough year and the growth trajectory you imagined. What would you be giving up if things stayed exactly as they are now?"

Response: That's the wrong question, I think. It's not about what I'd be giving up—I know what that list looks like. Less travel, fewer client dinners, probably working until I'm seventy.

The question is what I'd be giving up if I took the deal. And I don't think I can explain that to someone who hasn't built something. It's not about money or title. It's about... walking into a room and knowing that whatever happens next, I chose it.

Year-end push. Everyone's trying to close projects before the holidays, which means I've been in back-to-back calls since seven this morning. Just finished the last one—client wanted changes to something we finalized two weeks ago, and my first instinct was to push back hard.

Didn't. Let Erik handle the diplomacy while I drafted the revision scope. We'll bill for the extra work, client's happy, team's intact.

Small victory, I guess. Learning to pick battles.

Emilia asked if I was coming to the Lucia dinner tomorrow. I'd forgotten it was Lucia already. Said yes before I could think of reasons to say no. Sometimes that's the right approach.

The consultancy is going to make it through another year. Not where I wanted to be, but still here. Still mine.

Closed the office for the holidays today. Handed out bonuses—smaller than I wanted, but real. Anna cried. Erik made a toast that went on too long but meant something.

I don't know why I'm getting emotional about this. It's just another December.

But I keep thinking about Marcus's offer, which I finally declined this morning. Kept the email short—appreciated the thought, not the right fit, let's catch up in the new year. He'll understand, or he won't.

What I couldn't write in the email is that the offer made me realize something. I don't want what he has. I'm not even sure I want what I thought I wanted when I started this—the growth, the big clients, the industry recognition. What I want is to keep making choices. To wake up and know that whatever problems I face today, they're problems I chose.

The rough years are mine. The small bonuses are mine. The team that stays anyway is mine.

Drove home through snow again. Stopped at that overlook near Djurgarden, the one Sara and I used to go to. Sat there for a while. Felt okay.

Nudge: "You mentioned Sara. Is that weight you're still carrying, or something you've set down?"

Response: Set down, mostly. We split four years ago—she wanted more certainty than I could give her, and she was right to want it. I just couldn't be that person.

Stopping at that spot wasn't about missing her specifically. It was more like... checking in with a version of myself that made a choice. The choice cost me something. I wanted to remember that and still feel okay about it.

I think I do.

Third week of summer break and I'm already crawling out of my skin, spent the whole morning refreshing job boards for literally anything that isn't sitting at home, maybe I should just book a flight somewhere, anywhere, the walls are closing in.

Nudge: "What happened this morning that set this off?"

Principal Chen called about leading the new student orientation committee for August. I said yes before she finished explaining the scope.

Nudge: "You didn't even let her finish?"

Response: Honestly no. The moment she said "lead" I was already in. I've been rotting at home for three weeks with nothing to run, nothing to decide. My mom keeps asking why I don't just relax like a normal person during break. But sitting around feels like drowning. At least now I have something with my name on it.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. The orientation committee met today on Zoom and Ms. Liu, who's been at the school for fifteen years, kept interrupting me to "add context" every time I proposed something. Context! As if I don't know our own students. She literally said "Wei-Lin, you're still new here" in front of everyone when I suggested we move the welcome assembly outdoors to the courtyard.

It's my committee. Principal Chen asked ME to lead it. But every time I try to actually lead, someone older swoops in to remind me I'm twenty-three. I left banking so I wouldn't have to wait in line behind people who got there first. I thought teaching would be different.

Nudge: "Different how?"

Response: I thought... I don't know. That being good at something would be enough. At the bank I was fast, accurate, never made mistakes, but promotions went by seniority. I figured teaching would reward initiative—you put in the work, you earn the room. But maybe every institution is the same. Maybe I'm just chasing something that doesn't exist, some place where being capable matters more than being old. That's a depressing thought. I don't want to believe it.

Same day, different mood I guess. After that Zoom call and venting here I went for a long walk around the harbor, watched the fishing boats come in, and something clicked. Ms. Liu wasn't trying to undermine me—she's been running orientations since before I was in middle school myself, and she probably felt sidelined when Chen handed the lead role to someone who's been there less than two years.

That doesn't mean I was wrong to want to shake things up. The outdoor assembly IS a better idea. But maybe I came in too hot, treating the meeting like a presentation where I had all the answers instead of a collaboration where people needed to feel heard. My dad used to say I sprint before people have tied their shoes. He meant it as a flaw. I always took it as a compliment.

I think both can be true? I move fast because slow feels unbearable, but fast doesn't have to mean alone. I messaged Ms. Liu after the walk and asked if she wanted to grab boba tomorrow to talk through the logistics together. She sent back a smiley face. Small win. I'll take it.

The harbor smelled like diesel and salt and I took some good shots of the cranes against the sunset for the gram. Feeling more like myself now. Sometimes I just need to move to think.

Winter break finally started and I should be relieved. Instead I feel this strange hollowness. My cousin Rania announced her engagement yesterday at the family gathering—everyone was so happy, talking about wedding dates and apartments and furniture. I smiled and hugged her but inside I was somewhere else entirely.

I keep thinking about that email from my old friend Amir. He's starting an educational nonprofit in Morocco and asked if I'd consider joining for six months. Six months. The thought makes my chest tight in two directions—terrified of leaving my position, my apartment, my carefully built routine. But also something like hunger.

My mother would never understand. She still talks about how "irresponsible" Malaysia was. But I was 24 then. I'm 29 now. At what point do I stop blaming responsibility for my fear and fear for my responsibility?

Nudge: "What would you actually lose?"

Spent the morning organizing my savings spreadsheet. I have enough for eight months of expenses if something goes wrong. That number used to make me feel safe. Today it just felt like a number. Had coffee with my sister. She mentioned a colleague looking for a roommate, assuming I might want to save on rent. Didn't correct her assumption.

Nudge: "What changed about that number?"

Response: I think I realized I've been saving for an emergency that might never come. Or maybe saving so I'd have an excuse not to take risks. "Can't leave, I'm building my safety net." But the net is built. So now what?

First day back at school. Taught the same Nizar Qabbani poems I've taught for four years. The students were half-asleep. I was fully awake but pretending otherwise. Emailed Amir back asking for more details. Just details. Not committing to anything. Put my phone in my desk drawer so I wouldn't check it every five minutes.

Nudge: "What are you hoping the details will tell you?"

Response: Whether I can do this without burning everything down. I want to know if there's a version of this where I don't have to choose between feeling alive and feeling safe. Probably there isn't. But I need the details to prove that to myself.

Twelve-hour shift again. My feet hurt and my back is screaming at me. There was a boy today, maybe seven years old, admitted with pneumonia. His mother was alone with him — the father works in Abu Dhabi and couldn't come. She looked so frightened sitting by his bed, not understanding what the doctors were telling her. I stayed past my shift to explain everything to her in Arabic, to help her fill out forms, to just sit with her while her son slept.

My own dinner went cold. Khalid texted me three times asking when I would be home. I could hear the frustration in his words even through the phone. But how do I leave a terrified mother alone? How do I explain that to him?

I am tired in my bones. Not just from standing all day but from carrying everyone's worry. Sometimes I wonder if there is anything left of me at the end of the day, or if I have given it all away.

Nudge: "You mentioned Khalid's frustration — what do you imagine he was feeling when he sent those texts?"

Response: He was worried, I think. Not angry. Khalid has been married to me for twenty-three years — he knows who I am. But lately I have noticed something in his eyes. A tiredness that mirrors my own. Last week he said, "The children are grown now, Fatima. When will it be our turn?"

I did not have an answer for him. I still do not. Because every time I think I can step back, there is another mother with frightened eyes, another child reaching for my hand. And I cannot make myself walk away from them. But I am also afraid that one day I will look up and find that Khalid has stopped waiting.

Sunday. My day off. I woke up early anyway — the habit of years — and made mansaf for the whole family. Layla came home from medical school for the weekend, and Yousef brought his girlfriend Sarah to meet us for the first time. An American girl, very polite but clearly nervous. I watched my mother-in-law's face when Yousef introduced her. That slight tightening around her eyes. But then Sarah complimented her embroidered thobe, asked about the patterns, and I saw my mother-in-law soften.

The house was loud and full all day. Arguments about politics, laughter over old photos, my youngest Nadia showing everyone videos on her phone. At one point I stood in the kitchen doorway just watching them all. Khalid caught my eye and smiled at me across the room.

This is what I work for. This noise, this chaos, this fullness. When I am at the hospital giving pieces of myself to strangers' children, I am also doing it for my own. I want Layla to see that a woman can have both purpose and family. I want Yousef to know that love means showing up even when you are tired. I want Nadia to understand that our home will always be open, always warm.

My mother used to say: "A woman's heart is like a well. The more you draw from it, the more water rises." I did not understand her when I was young. I thought she was saying women should give endlessly. Now I think she meant something else — that love is not a finite resource. It grows when you share it. The exhaustion comes not from giving too much, but from forgetting to drink from the well yourself.

Nudge: "What does 'drinking from the well yourself' look like for you?"

Response: I think that is a question I am still learning to answer. My sister Reem keeps telling me to take a spa day, get a massage. She does not understand that sitting still makes me anxious. I feel most myself when my hands are busy, when someone needs me.

Maybe that is the problem. Maybe I do not know how to receive. Even compliments make me uncomfortable. But this is too much philosophy for a Sunday evening. The dishes are not going to wash themselves.

The boy with pneumonia died tonight. Ahmed. Seven years old. I was not even on shift but the day nurse called me because his mother was asking for me. I held her while she screamed. I did not know what else to do.

I cannot stop seeing his small face. The way he used to smile at me when I checked his temperature. He called me "auntie."

I came home and hugged Nadia so tight she asked if something was wrong. I could not tell her. I just needed to feel her breathing against me. Alive. Healthy. Mine.

Khalid found me crying in the bathroom later. He did not say anything. Just sat on the floor next to me and held my hand until I stopped shaking. Twenty-three years and he still knows when words are not what I need.

I chose this work. I chose to love other people's children as my own. But tonight the cost feels unbearable.

Had a one-on-one with my manager today where he brought up the new "technical design review board" they're implementing. Starting in January, any architectural change needs to go through a committee of three senior engineers before we can even start coding. I nodded along and said it made sense for quality control, but inside I was thinking about how this is the third approval layer they've added this year.

Part of me gets it. We've had incidents. There's regulatory pressure. The team has grown from 12 to 45 people and you can't have everyone doing their own thing. But I miss the days when I could just have an idea, prototype it over a weekend, and ship it by Wednesday if it worked.

Spent the entire morning in back-to-back meetings about the new microservice architecture. Everyone has opinions, nobody wants to make a decision, and we ended up exactly where we started. I finally just said "look, why don't I build a proof of concept this week and we can evaluate from there?" My tech lead looked uncomfortable and reminded me we need to follow the new process.

The thing is, I know the process exists for good reasons. Last year Tomas pushed that caching change without proper review and it took down production for three hours. But there's got to be a middle ground between chaos and paralysis. Came home irritated and Maja asked what was wrong. I said "nothing, just work stuff" because I didn't want to vent about the same complaints again.

Nudge: "You mentioned not wanting to vent to Maja again. How often do you find yourself holding back like that?"

Response: Probably more than I should. It's just that she's heard the same story before - Erik is frustrated at work, Erik thinks things should be different. At some point it starts to feel self-indulgent. She has her own stress with the kids' schedules and her job. I don't want to be the guy who comes home every day complaining about meetings and processes. So I just... keep it to myself, put the kids to bed, then tinker with my side projects until midnight. It's fine. It works.

Sunday dinner at Maja's parents' place. Her father asked me about work and I gave the usual "it's going well, keeping busy" response. What I didn't mention was that I've been quietly looking at job postings this week. Nothing serious, just... looking. There's a startup in Gothenburg that's hiring senior engineers and their tech blog posts suggest they actually let people build things without eighteen layers of review.

But then I think about what leaving would mean. We bought this apartment two years ago specifically because it's close to Maja's parents and the kids' school. My in-laws help with pickup three days a week. The kids have their friends, their routines. And honestly, the current job pays well, has good benefits, and the people are mostly nice. It's just the bureaucracy that gets to me.

Over dinner, my father-in-law started talking about how his generation valued loyalty to employers and how job-hopping is "a young person's disease." I just smiled and nodded and said something about stability being important. Maja caught my eye across the table and I could tell she knew something was off, but we didn't talk about it in the car ride home. The kids were tired and there wasn't space for that conversation.

Nudge: "You smiled and agreed with your father-in-law about loyalty, while quietly browsing job postings. What's it like holding both of those at once?"

Response: Uncomfortable, if I'm being honest. I'm not sure if I was lying to him or to myself. Part of me does believe in loyalty - I've given this company seven years and I've built things I'm proud of. But another part of me wonders if loyalty is just another word for being afraid to rock the boat.

The thing is, I don't actually know if I'd be happier somewhere else, or if I'd just find new things to be frustrated about. Maybe the startup in Gothenburg would turn into the same bureaucratic mess in three years. Maybe the problem is that I want to do things my way and the world doesn't work like that when you're part of a team. I haven't figured that out yet.

Submitted my first technical design document to the new review board today. It took me two hours to write up something I could have just built in four. Three diagrams, a risk assessment matrix, rollback procedures. The whole thing. And you know what? They approved it in the meeting with almost no changes. The tech lead even said it was "exactly the kind of thorough documentation we need."

I should feel vindicated but mostly I just feel tired. I did the thing. I followed the process. And now I get to actually code, which is what I wanted to do a week ago. Maybe this is just what being a senior engineer at a growing company looks like. You learn to work within the system instead of against it. On the way home I stopped to pick up the kids from football practice and my daughter asked if I could help her build a website for her school project. I said yes immediately. At least that's one thing I can still build without a committee.

App keeps glitching today, lost two rides because of it. My head's all over the place.

Nudge: "Sounds like a frustrating day. What's weighing on you most right now?"

Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay this month since Dad's physical therapy bills are piling up. Of course I said yes but then I looked at my account and it's like, where is this money supposed to come from? I'll figure it out, I always do.

Nudge: "You said yes right away, then checked your account. What was going through your mind in that moment between?"

Twelve-hour day yesterday and another one today. Holiday surge is good for earnings but my back is killing me from sitting in the car so long. At least I was able to drop off the pharmacy money to Mom on my way between zones. She tried to give me back half of it and I told her no, just keep it in case something else comes up.

Had a passenger tonight who was heading to the airport for some tropical vacation and I sat there thinking about how I haven't taken more than a day off in probably six months. Not complaining really, it is what it is. Just tired.

Nudge: "Six months without a real break. If you could take a day off tomorrow, what would you actually do with it?"

Response: Honestly? I'd probably sleep until noon. Maybe drive out to the mountains just to sit somewhere quiet without my phone buzzing. But then I'd probably spend the whole time calculating how much money I'm losing by not working, so maybe that defeats the purpose.

Sister texted asking if I'm coming to Christmas dinner at her place this year. I want to say yes but I'm looking at the schedule and Christmas Eve and Christmas Day are usually the best earning days because everyone else takes off. Last year I worked both and just stopped by Mom and Dad's for an hour between rides.

Part of me thinks I should just take the hit and be there for the whole dinner. Dad's been doing better but who knows how many of these we get, you know? But then another part of me is like, one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment. I don't know. I'll probably end up doing what I always do which is work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present anyway.

My sister-in-law made a comment at dinner about how she could never "just stay home." I smiled and said something about how it's not for everyone. Dan didn't say anything. Nobody said anything. I loaded the dishwasher after and just stood there gripping the counter.

Nudge: "What were you gripping the counter about?"

Response: I wanted to say something back. Something sharp. But that would've made dinner weird and Dan's mom was right there. So I just didn't. And now it's stuck in my chest.

I know people think staying home is easy or that I'm wasting my degree. I see it in the way my old coworkers talk to me at birthday parties, like they're being careful not to make me feel bad about my choices. But honestly, I don't think they could do what I do. Managing two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule, appointments, groceries, meal prep, all of it on a rotating loop that never pauses.

And yeah, I chose this. Nobody forced me. When I was pregnant with Owen we sat down and looked at daycare costs and my salary and it didn't make sense. Dan makes more. That's just math. And I wanted to be home with them during these early years, I really did. I still do. But wanting something and never hearing anyone say "good job" or "thank you" are two different things.

My mom called this morning and asked if I'd thought about going back to work part-time. I told her I'm fine. She does this every few months, like she's checking if I've come to my senses. I know she means well. I just wish people would stop treating my life like a problem to solve.

Nudge: "What would 'good job' look like coming from someone?"

Response: I don't even know. Maybe Dan noticing that the house runs because I make it run. Or my mom saying she's proud of me without the follow-up question. But I think the real thing is I want to believe it myself. That this counts. That I'm not just filling time until the kids start school and I have to figure out who I am again.

Playgroup today. One of the other moms asked my opinion on the new park hours and I just agreed with whatever she said. Drove home wondering why I do that. It wasn't even something worth disagreeing about but I noticed myself doing it anyway.

Nudge: "What would have happened if you'd disagreed?"

Response: Probably nothing. She would've shrugged and moved on. But in my head it felt like it would've been this whole thing. Like I'd be the difficult one. I know that's ridiculous but it's automatic.

Dan's mom called this morning to ask if we're coming to Sunday dinner. I said yes. She asked if I could bring a salad and the rolls, same as always. I said sure. She mentioned that Dan's brother and his wife are bringing their new baby, so it'll be a full house. I said that sounds nice.

After I hung up I looked at my calendar. Sunday is the only day Dan is home this week. I had been thinking about asking him if we could skip dinner and just do something as a family, the four of us. Take the kids to the aquarium or even just stay home and order pizza. But I already said yes. And backing out would be a thing. His mom would call Dan, Dan would ask me what happened, and I'd have to explain that I just didn't want to go, which sounds selfish when there's a new baby to meet and everyone will be there.

So we'll go. I'll make the salad and the rolls. The kids will run around with their cousins and it'll be fine. It's always fine. I just noticed that I committed us before I even thought about what I wanted. Like my mouth answered before my brain caught up.

Nudge: "What would you have wanted instead?"

Response: Just a quiet day. Nothing dramatic. But it's fine, really. The kids love seeing their cousins.

Same day, later. Dan called to check in and I mentioned Sunday dinner and he said "oh great, I was hoping we'd go." And something in me just deflated. Not because he did anything wrong. He didn't know I wanted something different because I never said it. I never say it.

I keep replaying the moment I picked up the phone with his mom. I could have said let me check with Dan first. That's a normal thing to say. But I didn't because saying that would've felt like making waves. And I can't stand making waves even when the water is perfectly calm and nobody would even notice.

I'm tired of being the person who says yes to everything and then sits alone in the kitchen feeling resentful about it.

My cousin Luc called to tell me his wife is pregnant and they just closed on a house in Barrhaven. Good for them. I told him things are going well with the courier gigs and he got quiet in that way people do when they're trying not to say what they actually think.

Nudge: "What part of that silence bugged you most?"

Response: Probably that he didn't even bother arguing with me about it anymore. Like he's decided I'm a lost cause or something. A year ago he would've pushed back, told me to finish school. Now it's just quiet. I don't know if that's worse.

Picked up a double shift on the app Thursday because rent's due. Did about fourteen hours between rides and deliveries. Ate a shawarma in the car between pickups around Plateau. Phone died at 9pm so I had to call it early anyway. Made enough to cover rent plus a bit extra.

Maman texted asking if I'm coming to Tante Diane's birthday thing in October. It's in Sudbury obviously. I said I'd try. She sent back a thumbs up which from her basically means she already knows I won't make it. I'll probably go though. Diane's turning seventy and she's the one who taught me to skate.

Nudge: "What happens if you skip Diane's seventieth?"

Response: I'm not going to skip it. I just said I'd try.

Switched my Tuesday route to cover the McGill area because a couple of the regulars tip well over there. Figured out that if I stage near the Roddick Gates between 11:30 and 1 I can grab three or four lunch deliveries without killing my legs on the hills. Small optimization but it adds up over the week.

Called Grand-mère after my shift. She was watching her game show and half-listening but that's fine, that's how she is. Told her about the McGill route and she laughed and said her father used to deliver ice blocks on Sherbrooke Street with a horse cart. She tells me that every time. I don't mind hearing it again.

Guy at a red light today rolled down his window and asked if I was hiring for my "company" because of the courier bag. Made me laugh. Told him it's just me and my bike. But it stuck with me after. I've been doing this almost two years now and I never once had to ask anyone's permission to take a day off or switch a route. That part still feels right even when the money doesn't.

There's something about the rhythm I've built here. Mornings I pick my own routes. Afternoons I can stop if I want to or push through. When I was in the business program in Sudbury every hour was accounted for by somebody else's schedule. I couldn't breathe in that. Maybe some people need that structure. I just know I'm not one of them.

Nudge: "When the money doesn't feel right, what does that actually look like?"

Response: It's not dramatic or anything. Just some weeks are tight. I figure it out.

Maman called tonight and it turned into a whole thing. She wants me to come home for Thanksgiving and also stay the extra week to help Uncle René move out of his apartment because his knees are bad. I said I can't do a week and a half off the app, I'll lose my momentum and the algorithm buries you if you go inactive. She hit me with the "family is more important than an app, Marcus" line and I kind of lost it.

I didn't yell or anything but I got sharp with her. Told her she doesn't understand how this works, that it's not like taking vacation days from a normal job, there's no paid leave, no one covers your shifts. She went quiet. I hate when I do that to her. She's not wrong that René needs help. I just can't be the one who drops everything every time. But saying that out loud to her felt awful.

Nudge: "You said she's not wrong. So who is?"

Response: Nobody's wrong, that's the problem. She's right that René needs the help and that family should show up. I'm right that I can't just vanish for ten days without it costing me real money I don't have a cushion for. It's just one of those things where both sides are true and somebody still ends up disappointed.
